stops.name: Hippodrome of Constantinople
stops.shortDescription: Once the sporting and social center of the Byzantine capital, hosting spectacular chariot races. Today, the square still features ancient monuments like the 3,500-year-old Egyptian Obelisk of Theodosius.

stops.name: Blue Mosque (Sultan Ahmed Mosque)
stops.shortDescription: An early 17th-century masterpiece with six towering minarets and cascading domes. It earns its nickname from the more than 20,000 handmade Iznik ceramic tiles that intensely decorate its interior walls.

stops.name: Basilica Cistern
stops.shortDescription: A massive, subterranean water reservoir built by Emperor Justinian I in the 6th century. Supported by 336 marble columns, it features two mysterious, ancient Medusa heads used as pillar bases.

stops.name: Hagia Sophia
stops.shortDescription: Originally built as a colossal Christian cathedral in 537 AD, later an imperial mosque, then a museum, and now a mosque again. Its massive dome was considered an architectural miracle that changed the history of architecture.

stops.name: Topkapi Palace
stops.shortDescription: The opulent primary residence and administrative headquarters of the Ottoman sultans for nearly 400 years. Its sprawling courtyards house sacred Islamic relics, imperial armory, and the secretive Harem.

stops.name: Gülhane Park
stops.shortDescription: Istanbul's oldest and most expansive public park, originally part of the outer gardens of Topkapi Palace. In spring, the pathways burst into vibrant color with thousands of blooming tulips.

stops.name: Istanbul Archaeology Museums
stops.shortDescription: Located just off the outer courtyard of Topkapi Palace, this museum complex houses an immense collection of ancient artifacts, including the intricately carved Alexander Sarcophagus.

stops.name: Sirkeci Railway Station
stops.shortDescription: The historic, beautifully preserved 19th-century terminal station of the legendary Orient Express, which brought wealthy travelers from Paris to the edge of the Ottoman Empire.

stops.name: Eminönü Square
stops.shortDescription: The chaotic, vibrant waterfront square acting as Istanbul's central transport hub. You can grab a famous 'balık ekmek' (fish sandwich) from the colorful boats rocking in the Golden Horn.

stops.name: Yeni Cami (New Mosque)
stops.shortDescription: Dominating the Eminönü waterfront, this massive 17th-century imperial mosque is a stunning example of classic Ottoman architecture, constantly bustling with worshippers and pigeons.

stops.name: Spice Bazaar (Mısır Çarşısı)
stops.shortDescription: A feast for the senses since 1660. Walking through its L-shaped corridors exposes you to the rich, intoxicating aromas of saffron, sumac, sweet Turkish delight, and roasting coffee.

stops.name: Rüstem Pasha Mosque
stops.shortDescription: A small, hidden gem designed by the master architect Sinan. It is uniquely and entirely covered, both inside and out, with some of the finest Iznik floral tiles ever produced.

stops.name: Galata Bridge
stops.shortDescription: A lively, two-tiered bridge crossing the Golden Horn. The upper level is packed with local fishermen casting their lines, while the lower level is lined with fresh seafood restaurants.

stops.name: Galata Tower
stops.shortDescription: A towering stone cylinder originally built by the Genoese in 1348. Dominating the Beyoğlu skyline, its viewing balcony offers an unforgettable, sweeping 360-degree panorama of Istanbul.
